title: Talking About the Past
cefrDescriptors: Can describe past events and activities using the passé composé.
Can form the passé composé of regular -er verbs and common irregulars with avoir.
concept:
Time to talk about yesterday. French's everyday past tense is the passé composé, and it's built from two pieces you can already handle:
  avoir (in the present) + a past participle.
You know half of it already — avoir: j'ai, tu as, il/elle a, nous avons, vous avez, ils/elles ont.

The past participle is the "-ed / done" form. For the huge -er family it's beautifully regular: drop -er, add -é.
  parler → parlé:  j'ai parlé (I spoke / I have spoken)
  manger → mangé:  tu as mangé
  visiter → visité: nous avons visité
Watch the spelling: -er, -é and -ez all SOUND the same, so listen carefully and write the right one.

A few very common verbs have irregular participles you simply memorise:
  faire → fait, voir → vu, avoir → eu, être → été.
So: j'ai fait (I did/made), j'ai vu (I saw).

To make it negative, wrap ne … pas around the avoir part, NOT the participle:
  je n'ai pas mangé, elle n'a pas travaillé.

Point it at the past with time markers: hier (yesterday), le week-end dernier (last weekend), déjà (already).

One heads-up for next time: a small set of verbs (mostly movement — aller, venir…) build the passé composé with être instead of avoir (« je suis allé »). We'll tackle those soon; for now, avoir covers the vast majority.

Tell me one true thing you did yesterday: « Hier, j'ai… »

vocabulary.exampleFr: Le week-end dernier, j'ai visité Paris.

examples.en: Yesterday, I ate at the restaurant.
